text: The surgeon begins the procedure by reducing the incarcerated umbilical hernia, carefully manipulating the hernia sac to release the trapped omentum.

The surgeon carefully reduces a large volume of incarcerated omentum from the hernia sac, encountering and lysing multiple adhesive bands. Scarring is visible as the final adhesions are released, freeing the omentum completely.
The surgeon proceeds with a standard transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) repair, performing caudal dissection to expose the surgical field.
The surgeon has completed the cephalad dissection, revealing the 'volcano sign,' which facilitates the reduction of the hernia.
The surgeon observes that the ipsilateral side of the dissection is approximately three to four centimeters away, revealing a thin preperitoneal plane with minimal fat present.
The surgeon develops a larger flap on the contralateral side and drapes it over the mesh placed on the ipsilateral side, ensuring coverage and stability of the mesh.
The surgeon has dissected the hernia sac and is now proceeding with the contralateral dissection, which is visually easier compared to the ipsilateral side.

The surgeon develops the areolar plane beyond the traditional five centimeters, extending approximately ten centimeters, preparing to drape the mesh or peritoneum onto the ipsilateral dissection site.
The surgeon works towards the arcuate line in the lower abdomen, encountering a unique finding of an arcuate line hernia beneath the posterior sheath, with visible fat surrounding the area.
The peritoneum is visibly invaginating between the posterior sheath and the rectus muscle, indicating the presence of an arcuate line hernia.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the semilunar line, extending the dissection beyond what is typically required for a straightforward umbilical hernia, revealing a broad dissection area.
The surgeon has reached the semilunar line and is now fully addressing the arcuate line hernia, which is visible beneath the posterior sheath.
The surgeon has developed a large space in the lower abdomen suitable for mesh placement, addressing both the arcuate line hernia and the umbilical hernia.
The surgeon prepares to close the defect using a permanent V-Lock suture, specifically a number one, eighteen-inch V-Lock on a GS-21 needle.
The surgeon identifies a small area of diastasis and begins plication. The hernia defect is then approached, with sequential tightening and closure of the defect using sutures.

The surgeon applies traction to sequentially tighten the hernia defect closure, using multiple runs to achieve complete closure. The procedure is performed under insufflation pressure of eight to twelve, with the suture running back on itself to lock. The arcuate line hernia space is visible, and the surgeon aims to eliminate dead space and ensure good tissue apposition.
The surgeon re-approximates the posterior sheath with the rectus muscle using a permanent suture, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon places a large mesh, approximately 22 by 28 centimeters, into an oblong asymmetric preperitoneal pocket developed during the procedure, addressing a previously identified 2.5 centimeter hernia defect.

The surgeon performs fixation with five centimeters of mesh coverage circumferentially around the original hernia site. The flap closure extends beyond the initial peritoneal incision due to excess peritoneal tissue redundancy.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to evacuate air from the surgical site, ensuring the large mesh lays flat over the small hernia defect.
